overall_summary: The Large Public Power Council supports faster, fairer, more predictable large load interconnection and supplements the record with a snapshot of more than 50 gigawatts of member large load projects and the tariffs and contracts used to serve them. Its central position is that public power utilities and states already have effective customer-protective frameworks in place, so federal policy should build on rather than disrupt this progress.

quotes.text: We share the Administration's commitment to energy dominance and specifically support improving the speed, efficiency, fairness, and predictability of large load interconnections.
quotes.concern: Supports faster, fairer, more predictable large load interconnection

quotes.text: its member utilities have long employed public power tariffs, special contracts, and bilateral service agreements to integrate large loads while protecting all customers from undue cost-
quotes.concern: Existing tariffs and contracts already protect customers from cost-shifting

quotes.text: a summary of the tariff and contractual frameworks those utilities are using to ensure large load customers pay their fair share
quotes.concern: Frameworks ensure large loads pay their fair share of costs

quotes.text: LPPC member utilities are employing a variety of customer-protective mechanisms, including dedicated substations, upfront infrastructure funding requirements, take-or-pay provisions, minimum billing demand charges, exit fees, and bring-your-own-power arrangements, to ensure large load growth does not burden existing ratepayers.
quotes.concern: Catalog of customer-protective cost mechanisms in use

quotes.text: Both approved tariffs and contract-based structures are in active use across multiple states, demonstrating that states and utilities have developed effective frameworks before any federal mandate.
quotes.concern: States and utilities already have effective frameworks without a federal mandate

quotes.text: We continue to underscore the importance of crafting federal policies that build upon, rather than disrupt, this progress.
quotes.concern: Federal policy should build on, not disrupt, existing progress

quotes.text: Requires large load customers to procure or contract for their own generation supply.
quotes.concern: Bring-your-own-power generation requirements for large loads

quotes.text: Defines the type of large load customer that qualifies, such as a minimum power demand (ranging from 5 MW to 75+ MW across LPPC members).
quotes.concern: Eligibility MW thresholds defining large load customers vary by utility

quotes.text: Requires the large load customer to pay upfront for dedicated substations, distribution upgrades, transmission lines, and other required facilities.
quotes.concern: Customer-funded infrastructure so upgrades do not burden ratepayers

quotes.text: Requires the customer to post collateral or demonstrate creditworthiness prior to construction.
quotes.concern: Financial assurance and creditworthiness requirements before construction

quotes.text: Bills the customer at a minimum demand level (e.g., fixed percentage of expected peak demand), even if actual usage falls short.
quotes.concern: Minimum billing demand and take-or-pay protect against stranded cost

bins.name: No cost-shifting to ratepayers
bins.description: LPPC stresses that its members use tariffs, special contracts, customer-funded infrastructure, take-or-pay provisions, minimum billing demand charges, and exit fees to ensure large loads pay their fair share and do not burden existing ratepayers. It presents these as proven mechanisms already protecting everyday customers.

bins.name: Protecting existing ratepayers
bins.description: The filing frames its customer-protective mechanisms as the way to ensure large load growth does not shift costs onto other customers. It catalogs dedicated substations, upfront funding, and other provisions as in active use to protect all customers.

bins.name: Financial assurance and deposits
bins.description: LPPC describes financial assurance practices, including required collateral or creditworthiness demonstrations before construction and upfront funding commitments, as standard tools its members use to ensure large loads can cover their costs.

bins.name: Defer to state and utility frameworks
bins.description: LPPC argues states and utilities have already developed effective large load frameworks before any federal mandate and that federal policy should build on rather than disrupt this progress. It positions existing state and local processes as the foundation any federal action should respect.

bins.name: Existing frameworks already work
bins.description: The comment supplements the record with evidence that approved tariffs and contract structures are in active use across multiple states, presenting them as proof the issue is already being managed without federal intervention.

bins.name: Bring-your-own-power requirements
bins.description: LPPC highlights bring-your-own-power arrangements that require large load customers to procure or contract for their own generation, insulating other customers from capacity cost impacts.

bins.name: Customer-procured generation
bins.description: The filing describes generation requirements and customer-owned supply arrangements among the protective tools members use to serve large loads.

bins.name: MW eligibility thresholds vary
bins.description: LPPC notes that member tariffs define qualifying large load customers by minimum power demand thresholds ranging from 5 MW to 75 or more MW, supporting the use of size-based eligibility to apply special terms only to the largest customers.

bins.name: Customer-funded grid upgrades
bins.description: The comment describes requirements that large load customers pay upfront for dedicated substations, distribution upgrades, and transmission lines so the upgrades needed to serve them do not fall on existing ratepayers.

bins.name: Faster, fairer interconnection
bins.description: LPPC supports improving the speed, efficiency, fairness, and predictability of large load interconnections and aligns itself with the goal of integrating new demand responsibly.

bins.name: Speed and predictability
bins.description: The filing supports faster and more predictable large load interconnection as a shared goal, while emphasizing it should be achieved through existing customer-protective frameworks.
